It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 90 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 3.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see clearly:
Row 22 (upper frame): `ccccccccccccccccccRccOOOOccppppccNccNcccggcccRcccccccccccccccccc`
- R at col 18 (left border)
- OOOO at cols 20-23 (O placed in slot 1)
- pppp at cols 26-29 (p placed in slot 2)
- NccN at cols 32-35 (L-shape)
- gg at cols 40-41 (empty slot)
- R at col 45 (right border)
So the upper frame has:
- Slot 1: cols 20-23 (filled with O)
- Slot 2: cols 26-29 (filled with p)
- Slot 3: cols 40-41 (empty, gray)
- The L-shape is at cols 32-35
I previously clicked at col 40 which is within the slot at cols 40-41. But it didn't work! Wait, let me re-read the result. The action was MOUSE(row=22, col=40) and it had no impact. But the slot is at cols 40-41. Maybe I need to click at a different row? The slot is 2x2, so rows 22-23. Let me try row 22, col 40 again... but it already didn't work.
Wait, maybe the issue is that this slot is NOT placeable. Looking at the upper frame:
- 2 filled slots (O, p)
- 1 empty gray slot (cols 40-41)
- 1 L-shape area
Maybe the upper frame only has 2 placeable slots, and the gray slot at cols 40-41 is not placeable (it's just a visual placeholder).
Row 36 (lower frame): `ccccccccccccccccccNcccggccccggccccggccccggcccNcccccccccccccccccc`
- N at col 18 (left border)
- gg at cols 22-23 (slot 1)
- gg at cols 28-29 (slot 2)
- gg at cols 34-35 (slot 3)
- gg at cols 40-41 (slot 4)
- N at col 46 (right border)
So the lower frame has 4 slots at cols 22, 28, 34, 40.
Total placeable slots: 2 (upper) + 4 (lower) = 6 slots.
But there are 7 reference colors. So either:
1. One color is not placed (the L-shape color N?)
2. One slot gets two colors
3. I'm wrong about the upper frame slot count
Let me try clicking on the upper frame's gray slot at (22, 40) with a different color selected. Actually, I already tried and it had no impact. So the upper frame really only has 2 placeable slots.
So 6 slots for 7 colors. The L-shape is N (green). Maybe N is the "connector" and doesn't need to be placed. So 6 colors (O, p, R, b, Y, M) go in 6 slots.
Current state: O and p are placed in upper slots 1 and 2. I need to place R, b, Y, M in the 4 lower slots.
Reference order: O, p, R, b, N, Y, M
Skip N: O, p, R, b, Y, M
Upper: O, p
Lower: R, b, Y, M

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Upper frame has 2 placeable slots (cols 20-23, 26-29), already filled with O, p
# Lower frame has 4 placeable slots (cols 22, 28, 34, 40)
# 6 slots total for 6 colors (skip N which is the L-shape color)
# Need to place: R, b, Y, M in lower slots

# Palette positions:
# R: col 9-12, p: col 16-19, N: col 23-26, O: col 30-33, M: col 37-40, b: col 44-47, Y: col 51-54
# Row: 57-60

actions = [
    # R in lower1 (col 22)
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 10},  # Select R
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 22},  # Lower slot 1
    # b in lower2 (col 28)
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 45},  # Select b
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 28},  # Lower slot 2
    # Y in lower3 (col 34)
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 52},  # Select Y
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 34},  # Lower slot 3
    # M in lower4 (col 40)
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 38},  # Select M
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 40},  # Lower slot 4
]
action(actions)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
